title: The Pentagon’s AI Strategy Has a Funding Problem
gist: The White House's June 2026 AI directives require command-level software bought with operations-and-maintenance dollars, but the Iran war has drained those accounts and the $87.6B emergency supplemental contains no dedicated AI software funding — putting defense-tech's near-term revenue at risk unless Congress adds an explicit line.

insights.claim: In June 2026 the White House issued two sweeping AI directives — a June 2 executive order and National Security Presidential Memorandum 11 — anchored by four pillars of adoption, adaptation, assurance, and accountability, but the June 24 $87.6 billion emergency supplemental included no dedicated AI software funding.
insights.speaker: Adam Lackey
insights.so_what: Strategy is set but the money to execute it is missing — the gating risk for defense-tech contract awards.
insights.locator: In the span of two weeks

insights.claim: AI software the president's directives require is bought with operations-and-maintenance dollars, and the Iran war has drained those accounts.
insights.speaker: Adam Lackey
insights.so_what: Ties near-term command-software revenue directly to a single congressional funding decision.
insights.quote: The software that those directives call for is bought with operations and maintenance dollars, and the Iran war has drained those accounts.
insights.locator: That is the problem in one line

insights.claim: Operation Epic Fury, launched February 28, had reached a $29 billion bill by May per the acting Pentagon comptroller, with CSIS later estimating incremental war cost at $34–42 billion.
insights.speaker: Adam Lackey
insights.so_what: Quantifies the unbudgeted shock crowding out modernization spending in FY2026.

insights.claim: Chief of Naval Operations Adm. Daryl Caudle said his FY2026 budget was unprepared for Epic Fury and is already cutting training exercises, flight hours, and recruit instruction, with funding pressure that normally arrives in September hitting months early.
insights.speaker: Adam Lackey
insights.so_what: Confirms real, immediate O&M crowd-out across the services well before fiscal year-end.
insights.locator: This strain is visible across the services

insights.claim: Despite a generational private-capital commitment to American defense technology, the sector still draws less than 1 percent of total Pentagon contract dollars, and most of these firms depend on government contracts to earn investor returns.
insights.speaker: Adam Lackey
insights.so_what: Defense-tech's tiny share of spending means marginal budget shifts materially move the sector's revenue.
insights.locator: Private capital has made a generational commitment

insights.claim: Because most fielded command AI tools are still bought with operations-and-maintenance dollars rather than the limited pilot software appropriation, AI software competes directly with training, maintenance, and operating tempo — and the commands that most need decision-speed tools are often least able to afford them.
insights.speaker: Adam Lackey
insights.so_what: Explains why AI adoption stalls in a budget squeeze absent a protected funding line.
insights.locator: This hits AI software at the command level

insights.claim: The administration built parts of its FY2027 modernization plan on the assumption of a third reconciliation bill with roughly $350 billion in defense funding, but top Republican appropriators have poured cold water on that plan, leaving the supplemental as the most realistic funding vehicle.
insights.speaker: Adam Lackey
insights.so_what: The reconciliation backstop is fading, concentrating risk on one supplemental bill.
insights.locator: The administration built parts of its FY2027

insights.claim: The $87.6 billion supplemental directs $67.1 billion to defense including $17.3 billion for operations and $21 billion for munitions, but the closest thing to AI funding is $5.1 billion for 'cybersecurity and autonomy' with no detail on which capabilities it funds.
insights.speaker: Adam Lackey
insights.so_what: Without a named line, supplemental money risks being diverted to training and maintenance, leaving command software last.
insights.locator: But while the White House’s supplemental request

insights.claim: The executive branch could fund AI adoption without Congress via OMB apportionment control over $152 billion in reconciliation funding the Pentagon plans to spend within FY2026, plus Pentagon reprogramming authority under the FY2026 appropriations act.
insights.speaker: Adam Lackey
insights.so_what: Alternative funding paths exist that could reach software vendors before September 30 even if Congress does not act.
insights.locator: The executive branch also has additional tools

insights.claim: The author is the COO of Onebrief, a Series D defense-technology company whose AI planning software is deployed across multiple combatant commands, and explicitly discloses a commercial interest in the AI software funding the piece argues for.
insights.speaker: Adam Lackey
insights.so_what: Weigh the argument as an interested-party case for replenishing command-software accounts.
insights.locator: As a provider of AI-enabled military planning software

insights.claim: If FY2026 closes without meaningful new AI and commercial-technology awards because operations-and-maintenance funding ran dry, the author predicts real damage to the defense-tech ecosystem that answered the president's call.
insights.speaker: Adam Lackey
insights.so_what: A concrete near-term catalyst to watch: whether new AI awards materialize before September 30.
